Workflow Dependency Analysis

Huffmaster AppSheet RACI Workflow · derived from the Workflow_Actions tab · 719 rows · 6 stages · 6 gates

⚠ Phase 1 — desk inference (Rules & Guardrails v1.0). Dependencies here are derived, not confirmed: structural edges (from Parent ID + Stages_Gates) are reliable; edges labelled inferred are a draft for the process-owner walkthrough. Note under Rule 1 that not every row is a process step — 46% are requirements, gates, events or outcomes (see the Automation Map v1.1), so read those nodes as attachments, not sequenced work. Gate error-vs-warning (hard stop vs advisory) is still undefined — a flagged gap for the walkthrough. Nothing here is committed scope.

What the data says

The Workflow_Actions tab has no predecessor column, so every dependency below was derived — structurally from Parent ID and Stages_Gates, and semantically from the action names. The result is a clean, schedulable graph with no circular dependencies.

Headline findings

Execution class distribution

Sequential — order matters Parallel — can run concurrently Independent — floats in the window Convergent — join point Divergent — fan-out, start first Gate — hard boundary

Stage & Swimlane Map

Each stage is a batch of largely parallel work closed by a hard gate. Click a stage to open it; click any process to see its dependencies. Red-edged processes sit on the critical path.

Program Critical Path

Read this as the spine, not the schedule. It is the longest chain of hard dependencies from the first committed process to the final gate. Everything else in the workflow hangs off it in parallel. Slip any node here and the whole program slips.

Longest internal chain per stage

How deep the sequencing actually goes inside each stage. A chain of 2 means almost nothing is order-bound — the stage is a flat batch of parallel work gated at the end.
StageProcessesCan start immediatelyFeed the gateLongest chainChain

Parallel Tracks

Work with no dependency linkage, grouped by the role that owns it. Concurrent Items is how many things that one role is holding at once inside a single gate window. This is the number that actually determines whether the stage lands on time.
Every parallel item in this workflow is resource-constrained. All 359 of them share an owner with at least one other parallel item. Nothing in the workflow sequences them, so they look free — but they will silently serialize on one person's calendar.
StageSwimlaneResponsibleConcurrentCapacity riskItems

Handoff Risk Register

Every dependency where the predecessor and successor sit with different owners or in different swimlanes. These are the seams — where work gets dropped, waits, or gets done twice.
FromSwimlane / RoleToSwimlane / RoleTypeStageFlags

Role Load

Total items owned, split by execution class. Peak concurrent is the worst single stage — the most unlinked items that role is expected to carry simultaneously.
Responsible roleTotal itemsSequentialParallelOn critical pathPeak concurrentWorst stageFlag

Convergence points

Join nodes — every predecessor must land before these can start. The six gates dominate: each one waits on every terminal process in its stage.
IDActionStageOwner# Predecessors

Definition conflicts

The same action name appears in more than one stage. Where the owner also differs, it is an unresolved question — is this a genuine recurrence, or a duplicate that needs collapsing?

All 719 Items

Every row from Workflow_Actions with its derived execution class, predecessors and successors. Click any row for the full dependency detail.
IDLvlAction / TaskStageSwimlaneResponsibleClassPredSuccFlags

How dependencies were derived

The source tab has no predecessor column. Everything here is derived. Each edge is labelled with the mechanism that produced it, so you can trust the structural ones and validate the inferred ones.
✓ Structural edges — read directly from the workbook
Hierarchical Rollup
702 edges. Every child Task/Subtask must complete before its parent Process can close. Taken straight from the Parent ID column. Non-negotiable.
Gate Convergence
98 edges. Every terminal process in a stage must complete before that stage's gate can be approved. Source: the Stages_Gates tab — "All required predecessor tasks are complete; assigned owner has reviewed readiness."
Stage Gate Transition
86 edges. A gate must be approved before any entry process of the next stage may start. Six gates, from Stages_Gates.
⚠ Inferred edges — need SME validation
Artifact Link
Sibling A carries a producing verb (Create, Generate, Determine, Identify, Set up, Draft, Source, Schedule…) and sibling B carries a consuming verb (Attach, Send, Review, Validate, Confirm, Update, Recap, Approve, Submit, Invoice…), and they share a noun. Example: "Update Scope of Work" → "Attach: Updated Scope of Work". The stronger inference.
Sequence Verb
Sibling B carries a consuming/closing verb and immediately follows A under the same Responsible role, but shares no noun with A. Weaker — flagged separately so you can review these first.

The Excel file's Inferred_Edges_To_Validate tab lists every inferred edge, lowest confidence first, with a blank Confirmed? column. Rejecting a row costs nothing — the structural edges stand on their own.

Execution classes

Sequential
Exactly one predecessor and/or one successor. Cannot start until its predecessor finishes.
Parallel
No artifact or output linkage to any sibling. Can run concurrently with its co-equal siblings inside the same gate window.
Independent
No linkage in or out, and no co-equal sibling to run alongside. Floats anywhere inside the stage window.
Convergent
Two or more predecessors must all complete first. The sync points.
Divergent
No predecessor, but unblocks two or more downstream items. Start these first — largest unblocking effect.
Gate
Hard stage boundary. Convergence of everything upstream, fork into everything downstream.

Integrity checks

Rows reconciled
719 of 719 source rows classified. Zero dropped.
Cycles
0 — the graph is a clean DAG. The workflow is schedulable as written.
Orphan edges
0 — every edge resolves to a real row.
Virtual nodes added
6 gate nodes (G1–G6), so gates behave as real join/fork points rather than labels. Marked virtual; not counted in the 719.
Derived from Huffmaster_AppSheet_RACI_Workflow_Rebuilt_Guide_Updated.xlsm → Workflow_Actions. Structural edges are reliable; inferred edges are a first draft for SME review. Generated 14 Jul 2026.
